title: Staff+ Time, Calendar & Meeting Architecture
description: How Staff+ engineers design their week to balance deep work, meetings, code reviews, 1:1s, and leadership activities. At the Staff+ level, your calendar is not something that happens to you — it is something you architect intentionally. This guide covers calendar blocking patterns, deep work protection, meeting hygiene, the weekly rhythm, and the specific time management adjustments SAP engineers need to make in the transition to FAANG's async-first culture.

sections.title: The Staff+ Calendar Challenge

description.value:
The single biggest shock for SAP engineers moving to FAANG is the loss of structured time. In SAP, your day is structured by meetings (project status, design review, testing sync) and you fill the gaps with coding. At FAANG, your day is unstructured by default — you create the structure. New Staff+ engineers often fill this void with meetings and by the end of the week have done zero deep work.

The Staff+ calendar paradox: As you get more senior, you have more meetings (design reviews, cross-team syncs, 1:1s, interview debriefs, promotion committees, recruiting screens) but you also need more deep work time (architecture decisions, design docs, code reviews for complex changes). If you do not intentionally protect your deep work time, it will be consumed by meetings. No one will protect it for you — not your manager, not your team, not the calendar tool.

The typical Staff+ calendar breakdown: (1) Meetings: 15-25 hours/week (design reviews, 1:1s, team syncs, cross-team coordination, employee development, interviewing, and ad-hoc syncs). (2) Reviews: 5-10 hours/week (code reviews, design doc reviews, RFC feedback, performance review drafts). (3) Deep work: 5-15 hours/week (writing design docs, architecture decisions, complex debugging, writing code for the hardest problems). (4) Async communication: 3-5 hours/week (Slack, email, doc comments). (5) Administrative: 2-3 hours/week (expenses, performance review admin, planning documents).

The problem with the breakdown: Deep work (the highest-leverage activity for a Staff+ engineer) gets the smallest time allocation. If you do not protect it, meetings expand to fill the available time. The most successful Staff+ engineers treat deep work as a non-negotiable appointment with themselves — blocked on their calendar like any other meeting.

SAP-specific trap: At SAP, your calendar is managed by project managers and team leads. You attend the meetings you are told to attend. At FAANG, you manage your own calendar. No one will tell you what meetings to attend. You decide. If you attend every meeting you are invited to, you will have time for nothing else. The first skill to learn: declining meetings without guilt.

sections.codeBlock:
# Staff+ Calendar Audit Template

## Track your time for 1 week
| Category | Mon | Tue | Wed | Thu | Fri | Total |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| Meetings | | | | | | |
| Code reviews | | | | | | |
| Deep work | | | | | | |
| Async comms | | | | | | |
| Admin | | | | | | |
| TOTAL | | | | | | |

## Target allocation for Staff+
- Deep work: ≥12 hours/week (3+ blocks of 4 hours)
- Code reviews: ≤8 hours/week (batch them, don't scatter)
- Meetings: ≤20 hours/week (protect the other 20 hours)
- 1:1s: 3-5 hours/week (direct reports, skip-level, mentors)
- Async: ≤3 hours/week (batch Slack/email 2-3x per day)

## Red flags
- Deep work < 5 hours/week → you're not Staff+ing
- Meetings > 30 hours/week → you're in too many meetings
- Code reviews > 12 hours/week → you're a bottleneck, delegate more
- No lunch block → burnout incoming

sections.title: Calendar Blocking Patterns That Work at Staff+ Level

description.value:
The most effective Staff+ engineers use calendar blocking — reserving specific times for specific types of work. The key is not just blocking time, but matching the type of work to the time of day when you are cognitively best suited for it.

Pattern 1: Maker schedule (deep work first). (1) Morning (8 AM - 12 PM): Deep work only. No meetings, no Slack, no email. This is when most engineers have their peak cognitive performance. Schedule your hardest work (architecture decisions, design doc writing, complex debugging) in this block. (2) Early afternoon (12 PM - 2 PM): Lunch, walk, recharge. Non-negotiable. If you skip this, you will crash by 3 PM. (3) Afternoon (2 PM - 4 PM): Reviews, coordination, and async. Code reviews, design doc reviews, responding to Slack/email. (4) Late afternoon (4 PM - 6 PM): Meetings. This is when your cognitive performance is lowest — perfect for synchronous communication that does not require deep thinking. (5) End of day — prepare for tomorrow: Review tomorrow's calendar, write down the top 1-2 priorities for the next morning. Best for: Engineers who are sharpest in the morning.

Pattern 2: The meeting sandwich. (1) Tuesday and Thursday are meeting-free (deep work only). (2) Monday, Wednesday, Friday are meeting days (back-to-back meetings in the afternoon, with morning reserved for deep work). (3) No meetings before 10 AM or after 4 PM. (4) Protected lunch block every day (12-1 PM). Best for: Teams that have intensive coordination needs but can concentrate meeting days.

Pattern 3: Time-boxed days. (1) Each day has a theme. Monday: planning and reviews. Tuesday: deep work on the hardest problem. Wednesday: coordination and meetings. Thursday: deep work. Friday: close out, documentation, strategic thinking. (2) Each week has a 'free day' — a day with zero meetings where you can go deep on a single problem. Best for: Engineers working across multiple initiatives who need to switch contexts by day rather than by hour.

The golden rule: Whatever pattern you choose, protect the first 2-4 hours of your day for deep work. Do not schedule meetings before 10 AM. Do not open Slack or email before noon. The first thing you consume in the morning sets the tone for your entire day. If you consume 'urgent but unimportant' messages first, you will react all day. If you consume 'important but not urgent' deep work first, you will lead all day.

sections.codeBlock:
# Sample Staff+ Calendar (Pattern 1 — Maker Schedule)

## Monday
08:00-09:00 — Deep work (hardest problem of the week)
09:00-12:00 — Deep work (focus block, no interruptions)
12:00-13:00 — Lunch and walk (no devices)
13:00-14:00 — Code reviews (batch all reviews)
14:00-15:00 — Design doc reviews + async responses
15:00-16:00 — Team sync + 1:1s
16:00-17:00 — Cross-team design review
17:00-17:15 — End of day: review tomorrow's priorities

## Tuesday
08:00-11:00 — Deep work (block with auto-decline for meetings)
11:00-12:00 — Code reviews
12:00-13:00 — Lunch
13:00-14:00 — 1:1s (direct reports, skip-levels)
14:00-15:00 — TL-PM weekly sync
15:00-16:00 — Staff+ design review (weekly)
16:00-17:00 — Interview debrief / HC meeting

## Wednesday
08:00-10:00 — Deep work (finish the Tuesday deep work)
10:00-12:00 — Meeting block (team syncs, cross-team coordination)
12:00-13:00 — Lunch
13:00-15:00 — Meeting block (design reviews, ad-hoc syncs)
15:00-16:00 — Open office hours (any engineer can book 15 min)
16:00-17:00 — Buffer / overflow / catch-up

## Thursday (same as Tuesday pattern)
[Deep work morning, reviews midday, meetings afternoon]

## Friday
08:00-10:00 — Deep work (strategic thinking week review)
10:00-11:00 — Weekly planning for next week
11:00-12:00 — Documentation, ADRs, design doc writing
12:00-13:00 — Lunch
13:00-14:00 — Personal growth: reading, learning, side projects
14:00-15:00 — Retro / postmortems / process improvements
15:00-16:00 — Buffer / overflow / low-priority reviews

decisionTree.prompt: Which calendar pattern fits your current situation?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: What is your biggest time management challenge right now?

branches.condition: My calendar is fully booked with meetings — no time for deep work
branches.path: Start with the 'no meetings before noon' rule. Block 8 AM-12 PM as 'Focus Time' on your calendar with auto-decline for new meetings. Set your Slack status to 'Deep work, will respond after 12 PM.' When someone schedules a meeting before noon, decline with: 'I have a focus block then. Available after 12 PM or on Tuesday/Thursday.' Your first week, expect pushback. Your second week, people adjust. By week 3, no one books before noon. People respect protected time when you protect it consistently.

branches.condition: I have time but I'm not using it well — I waste mornings on Slack/email
branches.path: Change your morning routine. Do not open Slack or email before noon. Put your phone in another room. Start your day with the question: 'What is the one thing I want to have accomplished by lunch?' Write it down before you do anything else. The first action of your day sets the direction. If the first action is reading Slack, your day is reactive. If the first action is the hardest problem, your day is proactive.

branches.condition: I'm fragged — context switching between too many different types of work
branches.path: Adopt the 'time-boxed days' pattern (Pattern 3). Each day has a theme: Monday = planning/reviews, Tuesday = deep work, Wednesday = meetings, Thursday = deep work, Friday = close-out. This reduces context switching because you can say 'I'll look at that on Wednesday — that's my coordination day.' Most context switching is self-inflicted — you check Slack during deep work, you review code during meetings, you attend meetings during deep work. Stop multitasking. Commit to the type of work each day demands.

branches.condition: I don't have a calendar problem — my time is fine, but I feel unproductive
branches.path: This might be a problem definition issue, not a time management issue. Do a 'time audit' for 1 week: track every hour in 30-min increments. Categorize: deep work, meetings, reviews, Slack, email, admin. At the end of the week, look at the deep work total. If it is under 10 hours, the problem is not productivity — it is that your time is being consumed by low-leverage activities. Use the audit data to eliminate or reduce the lowest-impact activities next week.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The most effective Calendar intervention is the 'no meetings before noon' rule. It is simple, binary, and forces the rest of your schedule to accommodate deep work first. Do not negotiate this — block 8 AM-12 PM every day as 'Focus Time' and decline any meeting that lands there. If you compromise this for 'just one meeting,' you will have meetings creeping into your deep work within 2 weeks.

sections.title: Meeting Hygiene — When to Meet, When to Write, When to Decline

description.value:
Meetings are the single biggest time sink for Staff+ engineers. A 30-minute meeting with 8 people costs 4 engineering-hours. In a 40-hour week, 15 hours of meetings is 120 engineering-hours — equivalent to hiring 3 more engineers. Meeting hygiene is not a minor optimization — it is a force multiplier.

When to meet (synchronous): (1) Decisions that cannot be made async after 2 rounds of discussion. If the back-and-forth in docs or Slack exceeds 2 rounds, escalate to a 15-minute sync to resolve. (2) Brainstorming or problem definition. Whiteboarding together generates ideas that async cannot. (3) Sensitive conversations — performance feedback, conflict resolution, disagreement escalation. (4) Relationship building — 1:1s with your team, skip-level meetings, cross-team partner introductions. (5) Celebrations — ship reviews, demos, and team events.

When to write (async): (1) Status updates — write a weekly update doc instead of a status meeting. (2) Design proposals — write a design doc instead of scheduling a design meeting. Use the meeting only for discussion after the doc has been read. (3) Decisions that need documentation — write an ADR or decision log entry instead of discussing in a meeting and then documenting later. This forces clarity up front. (4) Information sharing — company announcements, process changes, team updates. Send a doc, not a meeting invite. (5) Feedback on documents — request inline comments on a design doc instead of scheduling a review meeting. Only schedule the meeting if the inline comments reveal unresolved disagreement.

How to decline a meeting gracefully: (1) Not your area: 'I don't think I'm the right person for this discussion. I suggest [name] instead.' (2) Could be async: 'Can we resolve this async? I'll review the doc and leave comments.' (3) Too many people: 'With respect, this decision can be made by [names]. I'll trust your judgment and read the decision log.' (4) Bad timing: 'I'm in a deep work block now. Can we reschedule for [time] or handle this async?' (5) Recurring meeting no longer needed: 'I've been attending this for 3 months. I think my time is better spent on [project]. I'll drop off unless my input is specifically needed.'

The 'meeting cost' rule: Before scheduling a meeting, calculate the cost: number of attendees × meeting length × blended hourly cost ($150-200 for FAANG engineers). A 1-hour meeting with 10 people costs $1,500-2,000. Ask: 'Is this meeting worth $2,000?' If the answer is not a clear 'yes,' send a doc instead.

sections.codeBlock:
# Meeting Cost Calculator

def meeting_cost(attendees, minutes, blended_rate=175):
    """
    Calculate the cost of a meeting in dollars.
    blended_rate = average fully-loaded cost per hour
    """
    hours = minutes / 60
    # add 20% for context switching cost
    cost = attendees * hours * blended_rate * 1.2
    return round(cost, 0)

# Examples:
# 5 people x 30 min = $875
# 8 people x 60 min = $2,800
# 12 people x 90 min = $6,300
# Weekly 30-min with 10 people for 1 year = $45,500

print(meeting_cost(10, 30))  # $875 per meeting
print(meeting_cost(10, 30) * 48)  # $42K per year

# Meeting decision flow:
# 1. Can this be a doc? → Send doc, skip meeting
# 2. Can this be 15 min when planned 30? → Shorten it
# 3. Do all attendees need to be there? → Reduce list
# 4. Does this need to be this week? → Defer if not urgent
# 5. Does this need to be recurring? → Set an end date

# The 'meeting end date' rule:
# Every recurring meeting has an end date.
# Review recurring meetings quarterly:
# - Keep: meetings that generate decisions
# - Modify: meetings that have drifted from their purpose
# - Kill: meetings that exist 'because we've always had them'

sections.title: The Weekly Rhythm — Planning, Reviewing, and Adjusting

description.value:
A Staff+ engineer does not just manage their time day-to-day. They manage their time week-to-week, month-to-month, and quarter-to-quarter. The weekly rhythm is the operating system that connects daily execution to long-term impact.

Sunday evening (30 min): Preview the week. (1) Review next week's calendar. Identify: deep work blocks (are there ≥12 hours?), meetings (are there ≥5 hours of meetings that should be declined?), priorities (what are the top 3 outcomes for this week?). (2) Set 3 weekly goals — not tasks, outcomes. 'Complete the payment migration design doc' not 'work on payment migration.' (3) Identify the one hard problem that, if solved this week, makes the other problems easier. Put it in your Monday deep work block.

Monday morning (15 min): Confirm the week. (1) Review the 3 weekly goals. (2) Check for new meeting invites that conflict with deep work blocks — decline or reschedule. (3) Set today's focus: what is the one thing you must accomplish today?

Daily end-of-day (5 min): Reflect and prepare. (1) What did I accomplish today? (2) What did I learn? (3) What is the one thing I need to start with tomorrow? Write this down before closing your laptop. This 5-minute habit saves 30 minutes of 'what was I doing?' the next morning.

Friday afternoon (30 min): Weekly review. (1) Did I meet my 3 weekly goals? If not, why? (2) What was the highest-leverage thing I did this week? (3) What was the lowest-leverage thing I did this week? (4) What meeting should I decline next week? (5) What deep work block should I protect more aggressively? (6) What is the one thing I need to carry into next week?

Monthly (60 min): Strategic review. (1) Am I working on the right problems? (2) Am I spending time where I add the most value? (3) Are my projects on track for the quarterly goals? (4) What should I stop doing? (5) What should I start doing? (6) How is my energy level — am I heading toward burnout?

SAP bridge: 'In SAP, time management was dictated by the project plan. You knew what to do each week because the project schedule told you. At FAANG, you create the plan. The weekly rhythm is your project plan — and you are the project manager.'

sections.codeBlock:
# Weekly Planning Template

## Week of: [Date]

### 3 Weekly Goals (outcomes, not tasks)
1. [Outcome 1]
2. [Outcome 2]
3. [Outcome 3]

### Deep work blocks (target: ≥12 hours)
- Mon AM: [Problem/goal]
- Tue AM: [Problem/goal]
- Wed AM: [Problem/goal]
- Thu AM: [Problem/goal]
- Fri AM: [Problem/goal]

### Meetings this week
- Must attend: [list]
- Can decline: [list]
- Can shorten: [list]
- Can make async: [list]

### One hard problem for this week
[If I solve this, everything else gets easier]

### Friday Review
- Goals met: [Yes/No — why?]
- Highest leverage: [activity]
- Lowest leverage: [activity]
- Decline next week: [meeting]
- Protect more: [deep work block]
- Carry forward: [unfinished work]

sections.title: Deep Work Protection — Strategies That Actually Work

description.value:
Cal Newport's concept of 'deep work' — professional activities performed in a state of distraction-free concentration that push your cognitive capabilities to their limit — is essential at the Staff+ level, where the hardest problems cannot be solved in 15-minute increments between meetings. At FAANG, deep work is not a luxury — it is a requirement for the level.

Strategy 1: The 4-hour focus block. Block 4 consecutive hours (8 AM - 12 PM or 9 AM - 1 PM) on your calendar with the title 'Focus Time — Do Not Reschedule.' Set auto-decline for meetings during this block. Turn off Slack notifications. Close your email. Put your phone in a drawer. Use this block for the hardest problem you need to solve this week — architecture design, design doc writing, complex debugging, code for the most critical path. Four hours of uninterrupted focus produces more output than 8 hours of fragged attention.

Strategy 2: The review sandwich. Before starting a deep work block, spend 5 minutes writing down: (1) What am I trying to accomplish? (2) Why does it matter? (3) What does success look like? This 'priming' step ensures your deep work is directed, not just intense. After the deep work block, spend 5 minutes writing: (1) What did I accomplish? (2) What is the next step? (3) What questions do I need to answer? This 'closure' step ensures the deep work results in a clear output, not just mental fatigue.

Strategy 3: Deep work triggers. Your brain needs a signal to switch from 'shallow mode' (Slack, email, quick tasks) to 'deep mode' (hard problems). Create a ritual: put on headphones with specific music (instrumental, no lyrics), open a specific document or IDE project, close all browser tabs except the ones needed for the task. After 2-3 weeks, this trigger becomes automatic — the headphones alone tell your brain 'it's deep work time.'

Strategy 4: The 'one yes, five no' rule. For every deep work task you say yes to (the single most important problem), you must say no to at least five things that would compete for that time. No to checking Slack. No to answering email. No to the 'quick question' from a coworker. No to the meeting that 'will only be 15 minutes.' No to opening a browser tab 'just to check one thing.' Deep work requires scarcity of attention, not abundance of opportunity.

SAP-specific deep work note: SAP engineers are often good at deep work because they have experience with complex, multi-day tasks (debugging a 10-year-old ABAP program, designing a data migration for 50 tables, understanding a custom code extension). The skill transfers. The environment does not. FAANG's open offices, Slack culture, and meeting norms are more distracting than SAP's cubicle-and-project-plan culture. You need to build defenses that you never needed at SAP.

sections.codeBlock:
# Deep Work Protection Checklist

## Before each deep work block
- [ ] Clear physical desk (phone in drawer, notifications off)
- [ ] Close all browser tabs except the task
- [ ] Set Slack status: "Deep work — will respond after [time]"
- [ ] Write down: what am I accomplishing in this block?
- [ ] Write down: what does success look like for this block?

## During deep work block
- [ ] Do not check Slack (the status says you're in deep work)
- [ ] Do not check email
- [ ] Do not switch tasks (if stuck, take a short break, then return)
- [ ] Use the 'next action' rule: if stuck on one approach,
  write down the alternative approach and continue

## After deep work block
- [ ] Write down: what did I accomplish?
- [ ] Write down: what is the next step?
- [ ] Write down: what questions do I need to answer?
- [ ] Respond to any urgent messages (check priority, not chronology)

## Weekly deep work audit
- Total deep work hours this week: ___
- Target: ≥12 hours
- If below target: what meetings can I decline next week?
  What distractions can I eliminate?

adversarialResponse.challenge: "My calendar is controlled by my team and my manager. I can't just block 4 hours every morning — I have design reviews, 1:1s, team syncs, and cross-team meetings that are scheduled by other people. If I decline, I look like I'm not a team player."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "You need to be more assertive. Just block the time and decline everything." (ignores the real organizational pressure to attend meetings)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Two things: (1) Most people who say 'my calendar isn't mine' haven't actually tried blocking time consistently. The first week is hard — people push back. By week 3, it becomes the norm. Try it for 2 weeks and see what actually breaks, rather than assuming it will. (2) If you genuinely can't protect mornings (e.g., your team is distributed across time zones and morning is the only overlap), protect afternoons instead. Or protect Tuesday/Thursday as meeting-free days. The specific time matters less than having ANY consistently protected deep work time. Also, talk to your manager: 'I'm spending 25 hours in meetings and only 5 hours on deep work. My architecture decisions are suffering. Can we review my meeting load?' A good manager will help you cut meetings. A manager who doesn't is a signal that you need to be more aggressive about protecting your time — because no one else will."
adversarialResponse.pattern: try-it-before-assuming + manager-conversation — the most common reason engineers don't protect deep work is they assume it won't work before trying it. The second most common is they haven't explicitly discussed their meeting load with their manager. Combine a 2-week trial with a data-driven conversation about what your time should be spent on.

sections.title: Async Communication at Staff+ Scale

description.value:
FAANG runs on async communication — Slack messages instead of meetings, doc comments instead of hallway conversations, written status updates instead of stand-ups. For SAP engineers used to structured meetings and hierarchical communication, async is both freeing and frustrating. This section covers how Staff+ engineers manage async communication without letting it consume their day.

Batching vs real-time: The biggest async mistake is treating it as real-time. If you respond to every Slack message within 2 minutes, you are training your team to expect instant responses — and destroying your deep work. Instead, batch your async communication: (1) Check Slack 2-3 times per day: mid-morning (after deep work block), after lunch, and end of day. (2) Set expectations in your Slack status: '💻 Deep work — will respond at 12 PM and 4 PM. For urgent issues, @mention or page.' (3) Use Slack's notification schedule: mute notifications during deep work blocks. (4) For important messages that need a response, reply with 'Noted — will respond properly by [time].'

Writing async updates: (1) Weekly update — a 2-3 paragraph doc shared with your manager and stakeholders. Structure: What I shipped this week, What I'm working on next week, What I need help with, What I learned. This replaces the 1:1 status report and the stand-up update. (2) Decision notifications — when you make a decision that affects others, write a short doc or message: 'Decision: We will use Avro for the event format. Rationale: [3 bullet points]. Impact: [what changes for you]. Timeline: [when it happens]. Questions by: [date].' (3) Request for input — when you need feedback, write a focused request: 'I need input on the migration approach by Wednesday. Here is the one-page proposal. The specific decision I need help with is: should we migrate in one weekend or incrementally over 2 weeks? Here are the tradeoffs: [2 bullet points each].'

The 'time to respond' norms: (1) Direct messages from your manager or team: respond within 4 hours during business hours. (2) Direct messages from others: respond within 24 hours. (3) Channel @mentions: respond within 24 hours. (4) Doc comments: respond within 48 hours. (5) Code review comments: respond within 24 hours. (6) Email (yes, email still exists at some FAANGs): respond within 48 hours. Set these norms explicitly in your team's working agreement so no one is guessing.

sections.codeBlock:
# Async Communication Norms Template

## Team Working Agreement

### Slack
- Response time for direct messages: <4 hours (business hours)
- Response time for channel @mentions: <24 hours
- Urgent issues: @mention + 'URGENT' in message
- Do NOT @mention for non-urgent issues
- Slack status reflects availability (deep work = do not disturb)

### Design docs
- Response time for comments: <48 hours
- Request review by: tagging specific people, not the whole org
- Decision deadline must be in the doc
- If no comments within 48 hours, proceed by default

### Code reviews
- First review: <12 hours target, <24 hours maximum
- Follow-up review: <4 hours
- Author responds to comments: <24 hours
- Stuck reviews (3+ rounds): escalate to 15-min sync

### Email
- Response time: <48 hours
- If urgent, send Slack message instead

### The golden rule
Assume positive intent. If someone hasn't responded,
they are busy, not ignoring you. If you need a faster
response, escalate channels (DM → @mention → Slack huddle).

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Time management questions appear in Staff+ behavioral interviews as 'How do you prioritize your work?' or 'Tell me about a time you had too many competing priorities.' The best answers demonstrate: (1) You have a system — you don't just 'figure it out.' (2) You protect deep work aggressively. (3) You use data to make decisions about what to stop doing. An SAP engineer's answer: 'At SAP, I managed architecture decisions across 3 parallel projects. I used calendar blocking — 4 hours every morning for architecture work, afternoons for coordination. I tracked my time allocation weekly and adjusted. When I was spending too much time in meetings, I delegated code review to senior developers and used weekly updates instead of status meetings. This allowed me to focus on the highest-leverage design decisions.' This demonstrates systematic time management that translates directly to FAANG.
